purpose: Dual-mode permission popup for agent tool requests. Mode 1 (informational, backward-compatible): shown when an agent requests permission OUT-OF-BAND (e.g. classic Claude CLI in terminal) — shows 'Respond in your terminal to continue' with a Dismiss button. Mode 2 (interactive): shown when the harness intercepts a Claude SDK tool-permission request — displays toolName + formatted toolInput (file_path / command / path / first stringy value), Approve/Deny buttons, and an optional 'Always allow {tool}' checkbox that calls onAlwaysAllow(toolName) before approving so future calls of that tool auto-approve. Bypasses Modal.jsx base — implements its own .permission-overlay structure to give it distinct positioning and styling, including a warning icon header.

exports.name: PermissionModal
exports.signature: { onClose, message, agent, interactive, onApprove, onDeny, toolName, toolInput, onAlwaysAllow }

gotchas: Does NOT extend the Modal base — duplicates ESC handling and backdrop-click logic. If Modal's behavior is updated (e.g. focus trap added), this modal will diverge.

Interactive mode checkbox 'alwaysAllow' calls onAlwaysAllow BEFORE onApprove — order matters so that the persistent permission is registered before the agent receives the approval and proceeds with the next tool call.

formatToolInput's fallback (first stringy value) can leak unexpected fields — for tools with multiple string args, the displayed value is whatever Object.values returns first (insertion order), which may be the wrong arg.
The 'agent' prop is just a display string ('from {agent}') — the actual agent identity for routing approvals lives in the parent's state, NOT in this modal.

interactive mode showing detail string can be very long (e.g. full command) and the UI renders it in a single .permission-tool-input div without truncation by default — relies on CSS for overflow handling.
patterns: Single component handles two distinct modes via a boolean prop — simpler than two separate components but increases prop API surface.
Uses unicode escapes for icon (\u26A0 = warning triangle, \u2192 = right arrow) rather than emoji — consistent ASCII rendering across platforms.
